posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
I was able to travel down to Atlanta for another wonderful edition of Supper Club. We had an exceptional evening. So many wines in great balance. And a table in great balance, too. It’s a special kind of relaxation to have 4 hours and not be rushed with wines & people of this quality .

Champagnes

2017 Ulysse Collin Les Maillons Blanc de Noirs & 2014 Ulysse Collin Les Maillons Rose de Saignee

We had the unusual pleasure of a Ulysse Collin side-by-side. Different expressions of the same vineyard. Very enjoyable and also educational.

On the nose, I thought the wines had a real similarity in their pinot noir base. Both wines were very clean, aromatically. Elegant, not wild. The Rose lead with more light strawberry, of course.

In the mouth & palate, I think the 17 Maillons had more excitement & burst. Very composed mouthfeel. Some stonefruit, a touch of cream. The 2017 had a 90-minute pre-game open & re-close before arrival. I think it was fully releasing and showing a lot of fresh character.

The 14 Maillons Rose de Saignee was a little more reserved by comparison. Not as electric. Well composed in the mouth, and elegant. Some classy black currant. Unsure if different editions of this present as a little more wild or colorful. This was a very nice rose Champagne but perhaps missing some verve.

Les Montrachets

2014 Sauzet & 2014 Prieur

Once again, a legit Montrachet performance with the Atlanta crew. They have a touch. Both wines getting a 45-minute decant. The Sauzet got opened 2 hours in advance with some slox-ox. The Sauzet, in particular, kept getting stronger throughout the evening.

A beautiful contrast in style. The Prieur leapt from the glass as soon as it was poured. Very much in a rich, beautiful vein of luscious butter & seductive fruit. Very composed front to back. The lush style stays in balance. For me, this had a very Leflaive style & texture. Unabashedly rich & exciting, and just enough mineral expression through the mid-palate to keep the wine flowing through its progression and finish. A delight.

The Sauzet was my preferred, but I think that’s a style thing. Andrew’s got a way with Sauzet. This 2014 had so much structure, and its unwinding was a real thrill. Andrew was able to nurse his glass longer than the rest of us, so his notes can include a three-hour progression.

For me, a beautiful minerality lingers with this wine. So much structure and elegance. The white peach, gentle flowers & maybe a touch of honeycomb begins to flow from the glass as it warms up & relaxes. I think Andrew got a lot more honey by the end of the night. The minerality is top, top level. Gorgeously strong & gripping. But still a very smooth, easy to drink wine as it warms up.

We had a rich poached lobster with these two wines. A beautiful flight.

Intermezzo: 1959 Huet Molleux Le Haut-Lieu

A gorgeous course with a fun DIY Brioche, Jersey Girl melted cheese & baked apple accompaniment. A fantastic bring from Mike Evans. Listening to him present this wine & revel in its unique character was so much fun. This wine got a bit of a slow-ox to wake up.

I’ll keep my note short and let him expand. Very dark in color, a touch of candy in the nose along side the buttered nuts. The palate was less sweet than the nose, and with a deep complexity. The complexity stood out most to me. A delightful intermezzo opportunity, not opulent or exceptionally sweet, but rather a kaleidoscope of mature flavors and excellent texture.

Red Burgundies

First Flight: 2002 Vogue Musigny & 2002 Roty Charmes-Chambertin (+ a Blind)

Probably my favorite wine-oriented conversation on this flight. The Vogue was a pure stunner. Joyous, leaping from the glass with an easy intensity of juicy fruit. A touch of meat, but not as unctuously meaty as other vintages. There is a brightness to this wine and so much rounded fruit.

Mid-palate of utter pleasure. Silky texture but with a gentle electricity as it goes into the finish. This is a wine with so much energy and yet still manages to stay in balance on the finish. All that energy slides to the finish without any coarseness of bite. WOTN for some of us (but not all!). A joyous match with a really beautiful, thickly cut roast duck

Meanwhile, the Roty was stealing hearts across the table . A much more classic , restrained glass of Burgundy. Lush, lithe fruit on the nose (albeit with much lower volume than its tablemate), an excellent mid-palate texture, and more rustic, gripping finish. Very much a roast gamebird grand cru.

For my personal preference, I would put this a tier below the other Red Burgundy stars of the evening, but its classicism stole a few hearts.

For an interloper, Andrew brought the 2016 Roty CC to hang out with its grad student older brother. This wine showed magically! Very hard to guess this vintage (literally none of us did ). Very lush & velvet texture. Even getting some Rousseau mentions. For me, not quite that level of aromatics or lighter balance, but certainly a very sexy showing of lush quality. Probably the most overperforming wine of the evening.

Final Flight: 2005 & 2008 Mugneret-Gibourg Clos Vougeot

I was worried about the 2005, and thankfully: I needn’t have been. What beautiful wines

Funniest wine moment of the night for me was looking across the table and realizing I hadn’t heard from Mike Evans on these two yet. So I called out to him if he had any particularly discerning thoughts on the duo & the contrasts. He had this wry gleam on his face, a clear expression of These wines are really effing good. He spoke in complete sentences, because he’s a refined gentleman, but I’m going to stick with the expression. These wines are really effing good.

Both of these got a few hours of slow-ox. The 2005 showed well from the beginning, not closed or brooding. And, as you might expect, last sips were best sips. Wine is in a very good place and allows you to see it/approach it with different levels of roundedness the longer you let run through the evening. We had a lovely rare NY trip which was a perfect match.

Before the last sips, I’d have chosen the 2008 as my favorite, simply because it was a little more unusual, I think. More of a racier raspberry on the nose, a little more electric acid in the mid-palate, et al. But, by the end of the evening, the rounder, bigger cherry of the 2005 kept expanding and expanding into a richer experience. Hard to pick between these two. So gorgeous.

@Matthew_King started off this month by bringing the 2001 M-G Clos Vougeot to NY, and I’ve been benefiting from that karmic kickstart all March! A beautiful 2000 with @MChang and now these two Jewels. 4 for 4. Beautifully structured wines. Demanding attention but still approachable and with sufficient finesse to compliment their framing.

I’ve been a lucky man this month. I am generally a lucky man when amongst my wine friends, and I could not be more happy & grateful .

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
Great notes. Great wines.

I’ve always loved the Roty Grand Crus. Does anyone know the Roty family’s care regimen and the status of its old Charmes-Chamb vines and how they are doing? AFAIK they are still basically the, or certainly among the, oldest Pinot vines in Burgundy, planted in the 1880s after the phylloxera epidemic.

Maybe kind-sir @William_Kelley knows?

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Nice notes, young man!

I can’t say for sure but I think you sampled the 08 CV during our Marea dinner earlier this month … in between your squats!

IMG_31394284×5712 2.52 MB

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
(Chuckles) I did not, because I was respectful of being an extra wheel :). Those two Whites were killer enough. So I did not force you to go through all the Flights with me .

Getting to try that Leflaive both early & late was a blast. Amazing progression through the evening. Steve was spot on in his analysis.

posts.name: Rich Brown
posts.cooked: Great notes per usual my friend! Love hearing about that 59 Huet! I have a few Clos du Bourg Moelleux in the cellar from the same vintage and plan to pop one this summer

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked:
Nick, it was great to see you, thank you for organizing and for the notes. I too preferred the 2017 Maillons to the rosé for its greater complexity and depth, though the similarities were interesting.

I thought the Prieur was a very good but not great wine, it seemed to be lacking a gear relative to a number of other top white Burgs I’ve had over the past couple of years. The Sauzet was tight but still quite impressive, and though it suffered compared to my memory of the extraordinary 2010 from the last dinner, I think it will get closer with time.

The 1959 Huet was the Moelleux Premiere Trie and I was quite pleased with it. Still with plenty of sweetness but perfectly balanced by its acidity, a characteristic that is part of what makes these wines so versatile. It paired well with the apples and cheese and still has decades left in the tank.

The 2002 flight was magnificent, as the wines paired brilliantly with the perfectly prepared duck. I had a slight preference for the earthy rusticity of the 2002 Roty but completely understand the preference others had for the Musigny. Andrew’s 2016 was an interesting contrast with an elegance that reminded me of Chambolle. While very enjoyable now, it should become even more brilliant with age.

I found the Mugneret-Gibourg flight very close in quality. The 2005 surprised me with how open and approachable it was, but I appreciated the tart, red fruited charms of the 2008 as well.

All in all, a lovely and memorable evening with great food, wine, and friends.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: I’ve found most vintages of MG to be drinking pretty well right now. 13 Ruchottes probably could have used a touch more time, but wasn’t bad. Probably the only one that really needed a few years was 17 Clos Vougeot last May.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The vines are doing fine! Of course, there have been some replants over the years of missing vines, but the bulk of the parcels are still the original vine material.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
• 2014 Domaine Jacques Prieur Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Montrachet Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    Decant for 30 min. Elegant and well structured this has a voluptuous frame with great acid and a nice finish.
  • 2014 Etienne Sauzet Montrachet - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Montrachet Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    Decanted for 30 min. So tight on first open I was afraid it was off so I didn't decant hours earlier which it could have used. Continued to improve over the evening. Wonderful structure and texture, incisive, chiseled, great minerality, opened to reveal white peach blossom with a touch of honey. Amazing wine.
  • 1959 Domaine Huet Vouvray Moelleux 1ère Trie Le Mont - France, Loire Valley, Touraine, Vouvray (3/29/2025)
    Deep caramel color. Complex nose; minimal sweetness with lovely acid. Very balanced. Paired wonderfully with the brioche/cheese/apple dish. A joy to drink, but one that can go straight to your head in the morning. 😂
  • 2002 Domaine Comte Georges de Vogüé Musigny Cuvée Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Musigny Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    Another wonderful bottle and possibly even better than the bottle 2 years ago. Nose erupts from the glass and in a perfect place with resolved, smooth tannins and a deep, layered palate. (95 points)
  • 2002 Domaine Joseph Roty Charmes-Chambertin Très Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    PnP. Crimson edge. Focused fruit nose. Mature notes on the palate. Tannins are a bit rustic.
  • 2016 Domaine Joseph Roty Charmes-Chambertin Très Vieilles Vignes - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Charmes-Chambertin Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    Double decant 4 hours early. Deep and dark. Powerful wine with depth and body, but lovely smooth tannins. Fruit forward and clearly a warmer vintage. Served blind and most guessed 2017 and no one guessed Roty.
  • 2005 Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg Clos Vougeot - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Clos Vougeot Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    Decanted. Wonderful wine in a great place. Deep and rich fruit with perfect structure. Long life ahead but drinking so much better than many 05s, which don't seem near ready. Kept coming back to this one as it continued to develop in the glass.
  • 2008 Domaine Georges Mugneret-Gibourg Clos Vougeot - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Clos Vougeot Grand Cru (3/29/2025)
    Decanted. More acid forward and subdued fruit compared to the 05. Perfectly nice wine, but felt like it didn't have the stuffing tonight.

Four hours on the double decant, huh? I feel like I missed that detail on the Blind .

I’ve got to be more disciplined in the Questions I ask & really use the details :). Beautiful wine tho.
